The downside to this is that we don't know the sensor will kick off these jobs which prevents us from associating them in the UI. For the use case that inspired this of a sensor launching any from a large set of jobs I think thats fine, but theres nothing in the API preventing someone from using this pattern for a sensor that always fires the same job but having a degraded UI experience of those entities no longer being associated. Docs & examples steering people in the right direction might be all thats needed, but i do think it warrants some consideration. A more aggressive approach would be to have an explicit "any job"  opt-in on the sensor, which feels like a lot to me.

Thoughts:

- I agree the current state of things potentially breaking as soon as something calls context.instance is not great.

- Requiring the user code server to be able to load the instance seems like a reasonable requirement to me - I think we used to have that, and removed it when we split the helm chart out into a user code helm chart and a system helm chart. With the benefit of hindsight I think allowing user code to work without an instance there was probably an error since you need to be able to load the instance anyway in order to do lots of things. Loading the instance once on user code server startup and passing it through / always being able to assume that it exists and has loaded already would be a lot simpler then all these lazy-loaded instance ref shenanigans (and probably more performant too - right now I believe we are creating a separate DagsterInstance object on every tick).

- it would be easier to guarantee that the instance will always load if we moved to a UserCodeLauncher model where dagster is responsible for spinning up the user code servers and setting all the needed secrets on them, this is how cloud works already today.

- I don't actually think optional args is the core problem here, that may be a problem for other reasons but the arg was set in the case where it broke for the user, it just didn't load when it instantiated because a secret was missing.

- the other angle you could take here is threading the lazy-loading even further down into the resource generation code, and have it wait to load the instance until there's a resource that actually needs to access the instance in its initialization function (I would guess that these are fairly rare). But that is probably doubling down on a bad setup.

- Overall I think we should land this and have a plan for a future where the instance is loaded once always on gRPC server startup

Hello @hemanth-silabs 

Thank you for this contribution, it's an important step towards more secure products! Good work!

I have an overall question about architecture. Right now the code is sliced with ifdefs in many places to choose between PSA API and mbedTLS API.

Haven't you considered only using the PSA API (whenever possible)? As far as I understand, we can update the mbedTLS version to one that supports PSA API (e.g. 2.25 from TF-M 1.3) - I have it done on a branch and can contribute. Since for non-TF-M type of devices, PSA API implementation translates code to mbedTLS API under the hood anyway. For example the `psa_hash_update` will call `mbedtls_sha256_update_ret` - [code](https://github.com/ARMmbed/mbedtls/blob/v2.25.0/library/psa_crypto.c#L2655). The only caveat is that for such non-TF-M devices, we would need to implement ITS API for the key-by-reference usage.
Thanks! 👍🏼

Unfortunately, I think that we're going to need to go in another direction with this -- the Note and Chord objects are too strongly tied to having pitches attached to them to change that this late in music21's history.  Notationally, unpitched notes and pitched notes are very similar, but from an analytical perspective, having some notes whose .ps is meaningless, or Chords where "isTriad" doesn't mean that it sounds like a triad, is going to cause too many problems.

I think that the note.Unpitched object needs to be restored OR something similar moved to percussion.py -- we probably need to insert some layer between NotRest (which I wish I had called something like Stemmable, but it is too late now) and Chord -- ChordBase or something, from which a PercussionChord or something like that appears.

In general, when we go in a different direction, don't reuse names, so we shouldn't create something called Unpitched in another module that functions differently from the previous Unpitched object.

My general concern here is that `--status` implies that it checks a running process whereas this is a prettier version of `--info`. I do see a value to giving users a way to do this. However, could we do this instead by changing the output of `--info`?

The only actual "request to change" are the magic numbers → named constants and expanding the names of those constants. Comments on style are just generally to be taken into consideration, not a must-have for this PR.

- need to deprecate module-level constants, looks like it could get hacky, but our next release might drop 3.6 so we may be able to use PEP 562 for this: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/45744919/subclassing-module-to-deprecate-a-module-level-variable-constant
- this should be reflected in the documentation as well, for example in https://github.com/python-gitlab/python-gitlab/blob/main/docs/gl_objects/access_requests.rst
- I haven't given it any thought but some basic tests might be useful 😀

Some comments

Discussed offline moving liveness probes to readiness. We've observed k8s killing users' Dagit pods when under load because the liveness probe fails. Readiness would be much better here because it would just stop sending requests to Dagit until it recovers. The risk is that if the Dagit process does somehow become wedged, it won't be restarted by K8s. We could consider an extremely relaxed liveness probe that should never fail except if the process is wedged, but absent of reason to believe that can happen, I don't think it's necessary.

Our liveness probes were introduced 2 years ago: https://dagster.phacility.com/D3531

It makes sense to me to remove the daemon's liveness probe. We could consider saving the liveness->readiness probes for 0.14, but it's not a super user facing change (we hope).

If we're going to introduce readinessProbes for real, we can probably skip this step. I'm happy to put a diff up

I have a couple of comments, most notably that we should avoid in-place operations in the input argument, I already had subtle bugs in the past because of this (imagine your results after the 1st epoch being completely wrong).

I also have a meta question that I would like to discuss here: we originally discussed about adding 2 conversion functions (`xyxy_to_xywh` and the other way around), but we also added the `cxcywh_to_xyxy` variants as well.
This brings the question of the scalability of the approach, as for each new format it adds at least 2 new functions (or ~ `2 * (n ** 2 - (n - 1) ** 2)` if we do the full conversion matrix).
For reference, [Detectron2 uses a `BoxMode`](https://github.com/facebookresearch/detectron2/blob/cb7e86f821f0bedb3da8f1e0a527dd821d8e5f7e/detectron2/structures/boxes.py#L13-L130) class to represent / implement the conversion types, which lets it handle the conversions as it wishes, with only a single entry-point.

I'm not advocating for using `BoxMode` (or something like this), but my original idea was that we would only be adding support for `xyxy` and `xywh`, which is still manageable.

I'm looking forward to your thoughts

Well done on creating a pull request! Roughly the same feature was proposed before by @kunif in #91. Unfortunately, his work is unfinished, but there are some things he did right: - He used `-p` as a short option instead of `-t`. `-p` has the advantage that it doesn't conflict with existing options of `pip list` and `pip install`. - He dynamically sized the column widths, so the data always fit comfortably in the table. - He ensured that the way the available updates are displayed (raw, wordy or tabular) was (mostly) independent of the way they are installed (automatically, interactively or not at all). This is not the case in the current version of your proposal: you cannot have a table and automatic installation at the same time. - He documented the new feature in the README. I suggest you read and learn from #91, including the comments, and also reuse the work that was already done over there, including the example code that I wrote in the comments. One particular way in which you might approach this, is as follows: 1. Add @kunif's fork as a remote to your local clone.
`git remote add -f kunif https://github.com/kunif/pip-review.git` 2. Create a new branch, based on @kunif's branch. This enables you to build on top of their hard work. Also, when your pull request is accepted, @kunif will automatically be credited as well.
`git checkout -b table-option kunif/add-preview-options` 3. Make the changes I suggested in #91. You are welcome to just blatantly copy the code I wrote. Commit these changes before continuing. 4. Make any other changes you want to make. Maybe you want to change `--preview` into `--table`. Maybe you can find a way so that the extra `-P` option is not needed for people who want a table and no installation (arguably, this is what should happen by default when only the `-p` option is passed, which is also how your current proposal behaves). Commit at least once along the way. 5. Push your `table-option` branch to your fork. 6. Update the current pull request to request that your `table-option` branch is merged instead of your `develop` branch. Failing that (if the option is not available, or it does not work for whatever reason), submit a new pull request instead. Some more comments below. Good luck and please let me know if you have any questions!

Hey, first of all thanks a lot for this PR, it's great to be able to extend Audio's features while staying compatible with the cog, and I have been waiting for this, so I was excited to try it out!

This PR is **big**, and I want to test it properly on my bot. I will explain what I'm gonna do in the following days/months:

## Our blind test events

I wanted to try the API by writing a blind test cog. If you don't know, a blind test is basically throwing some songs with the bot, and people have to try to guess the music first.

We use the Audio cog for these events a lot and it's working well. However, it's a horrible mess to manage, we usually need one animator who does the talk, and someone else for managing the bot. Imagine the following situation : 

- You have between 10 and 40 people listening in a voice channel and talking in a text channel, members that you have to moderate, and watch their answers (it's really spammy)
- While watching that channel, you have to manage the bot. The playlist feature is not adapted to this event (we don't want the bot to skip over to the next song automatically, and skipping starts the song automatically)
- So we're queuing the songs one by one. !play, !stop, !play, !stop, but that's not all!
- When the animator is talking, you have to lower the bot's volume so he can be heard. !volume 20, !volume 70, !volume 20, !volume 70, ...

Mix the command mess, the moderation in that active channel, the talking, watching for answers, writing the scores in a separate doc, also finding the next song link... And that's how we currently manage blind tests, a lot of work that could be simplified.

## The blind test cog

So I had the idea of making things easier with the bot for long, but the components and audio API are what motivated me to finally do it!

It's a player. With buttons. You load a playlist, then you shall only have to use buttons, and no one talks/enter commands.

Here's a demo video. I know, it's french, but you should mostly understand it. (Buttons when clicking on the menu mean: bumpskip, bump, remove, insert song after x).

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/23153234/150119026-9c8e269e-a68b-4273-b360-ffa738879446.mp4

## The issues I had so far

Lot of them. Then none. If you saw my conversation on Discord, you saw that I had some issues, well I couldn't reproduce any of them :P 

I will report what I have in the future, but for now it's pretty stable, and I can fall back to the Audio cog's commands easily. I guess the issues caused in the beginning were due to my intense testing and let lavalink in a weird state.

## How things will go

I'm gonna move Botnol (99 servers, handles a 27k members server alone) to dpy2 and audio API from now on for Audio features, and will report issues, both from the API and the Audio cog.

This is just a comment review, but things started well! If it's still stable after some time, I will approve the PR and comment the experience I had. Thanks a lot for your hard work, I hope this will be merged soon!

----

Oh btw, I published my blind test cog, you can see it here : https://github.com/laggron42/Laggrons-Dumb-Cogs/tree/blind_test/alpha/blindtest

Doesn't the `*Scheduler` already completely determine the makeup of the batches?

I seems to me that there is a lot of machinery to make sure an epoch has a defined length. Do we lose a lot if we just say "If you're doing multi-task learning, all iterators cycle ad infinitum, and you have to specify `instances_per_epoch` all the time?

The way it is now, the shortest iterator determines the epoch length, though not exactly, since it depends on what the scheduler does. When you specify `instances_per_epoch`, the story is different though, and now it's determined by the sampler, where you can only change things at the beginning of the epoch.

What's supposed to happen if the sampler says we should use only `"task_a"`, and the scheduler only schedules instances from `"task_b"`? What will happen in this code is that the epoch ends up with 0 instances, which will be surprising. The problem is that I can specify that preference in two places, and the two places can contradict each other.

The concept of an "epoch" as the complete training set doesn't really make sense in this setting. It only works out that way in a few special cases. I don't think we have to go so far out of our way to preserve it.

Can't we instead create an `ast.Visitor`?

Something like:
```py
from _ast import AnnAssign, Expr
from typing import Any
from pydantic import BaseModel
import inspect
import ast


class Model(BaseModel):
    a: int
    """My docstring."""
    b: int = 1
    """Docstring"""
    c: int = 1
    # This doesn't get to the AST.


class Visitor(ast.NodeVisitor):
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        super().__init__()

        self.target: str | None = None
        self.attrs: dict[str, str] = {}

    def visit_AnnAssign(self, node: AnnAssign) -> Any:
        if isinstance(node.target, ast.Name):
            self.target = node.target.id

    def visit_Expr(self, node: Expr) -> Any:
        if isinstance(node.value, ast.Str):
            docstring = inspect.cleandoc(node.value.s)
            if self.target:
                self.attrs[self.target] = docstring
            self.target = None


source = inspect.getsource(Model)
tree = ast.parse(source)

visitor = Visitor()
visitor.visit(tree)
print(visitor.attrs)
```

I'm pretty confused by this PR. Can you fill out summary and motivation?

Code-wise it looks good, `HTTPIterator()` instances are instantiated with the new `page_size` argument. :+1: I only found missing license headers and a punctuation nit.

Two observations to discuss:
- `RowIterator` subclasses HTTPIterator, but does not pass `page_size` to it in [\_\_init\_\_()](https://github.com/googleapis/python-bigquery/blob/dea2402ef62bcc00f2a392b16330a595db38ffb7/google/cloud/bigquery/table.py#L1467-L1478), although it does store it a few lines after, overriding the value the superclass sets. Should we nevertheless explicitly pass `page_size` to the superclass in case the latter's logic changes in a non-trivial way?
- The new `google-api-core` version pin is narrowed. We had problems in the past when different clout libraries pinned incompatible version ranges, causing conflicts, thus we tend to keep the ranges wide, if possible.
A quick glance over the core issue tracker does not reveal anything significant that would require capping the `python-api-core` version in other libraries, thus it's probably fine, just mentioning it as a sanity check.

@tswast Are you aware of any current issues in `api-core` that require a temporary version cap in any of the other libraries?

I appreciate the effort but I'd recommend consulting on the design and desirability of such an impactful change.

In the issue, you wrote
> I need to get RawHeaders from BodyPartReader and from MultipartReader

But as maintainers, we must consider what an average user needs, not one in a million. Any change like this will affect all the users and, of course, we wouldn't want such a penalty on them.

Moreover, it's best to explain your motivation with something more than "I want this" because the solution you invented may be architecturally wrong and nobody would be able to judge whether what you're proposing is even the right thing to do. Start with what you're attempting to solve with all the details and constraints, and code snippets. And only then follow up with how you envision that, including an explanation of why you think this proposal is good. It's also recommended to think about whether anybody besides you would benefit from this and whether it would be harmful in some cases. Of course, we'll help with the latter during the discussion/reviews but it's always preferable to learn such things before investing much time/effort into the implementation.

> I need to generate ICAP Response. ICAP packet consist of HTTP request or (and) HTTP response.

aiohttp is an HTTP server (and a client for that matter). Supporting other protocols is usually out of scope. And since we care a lot about the performance and memory consumption penalties, this is something we take into account when deciding whether the proposal is worthy.

I've marked some problematic changes in the comments below but besides that, I'm going to leave this for @asvetlov to accept or reject.

P.S. When filing issues and PR descriptions, it's a good tone to actually fill them out. Otherwise, you're forcing the reviewers to re-ask those same questions, increasing the latency of getting to the final decision.

The method should not return `False` in case the service is not available, because this would be an ambiguous result. Also the method returns `None` in case that there is no "NewEnable" entry in the dictionary provided by `call_action()`: in a boolean context `None` would interpreted as `False` masking the real error.

Instead it should be implemented like:

```python
@property
def upnp_enabled(self):
    """
    Returns a boolean whether upnp is enabled or raises a
    FritzServiceError in case the service is not available.
    """
    status = self.fc.call_action("X_AVM-DE_UPnP1", "GetInfo")
    return status["NewEnable"]
```
The first line will raise a `FritzServiceError` if the API is not provided and the second line a `KeyError` in case of a corrupted dictionary. Both errors are unlikely, however this is a clean separation and the return value is just about the upnp-setting.

In general though, I'm not sure why we want to have a random color palette by default. I fully agree it is better than using only white for everything, but I'm not sure why randomizing the colors is better than fixed ones. Apart from the non-determinism, by generating the colors at random we also risk using very similar colors for different classes, which can be confusing. It is actually not easy to have multiple colors that are easily distinguishable by humans. I suggest we hardcode color maps such as [this one](https://colorbrewer2.org/#type=qualitative&scheme=Paired&n=12). It "only" defines colors for 12 classes. If we need more, we can always supplement with randomly generated afterwards.

IIUC, we are only using `.as_subclass(torch.Tensor)` here to avoid `__torch_function__` here, correct? If yes, why not simply use the context manager that disables this?

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/07ae61bf9c21ddd1d5f65d326aa9636849b383ca/torchvision/prototype/features/_feature.py#L102-L103

like

```py
with DisableTorchFunction():
    return self.shape
```

Better yet, instead of adding properties for all these attributes manually, why not overwrite `__getattribute__` to do this for us?

```py
    _NO_TORCH_FUNCTION = {
        "shape",
    }

    def __getattribute__(self, item):
        with DisableTorchFunction() if item in type(self)._NO_TORCH_FUNCTION else contextlib.nullcontext():
            return object.__getattribute__(self, item)
```

Test:

```py
import unittest.mock

import torch
from torchvision.prototype import features

image = features.Image(torch.rand(3, 16, 16))

with unittest.mock.patch(
    "torchvision.prototype.features._Feature.__torch_function__",
    side_effect=AssertionError,
):
    image.shape
```

I have not benchmarked this. If it is as fast or faster than your suggestion, I would prefer this for several reasons:

1. We don't need to add properties manual, which is harder to maintain.
2. Subclasses can extend `_NO_TORCH_FUNCTION` or whatever we call it to also avoid `__torch_function__` calls on the metadata they are adding, i.e. `Image.color_space`.
3. Since we don't need to unwrap anymore, we can also avoid the issues @datumbox mentioned in https://github.com/pytorch/vision/pull/6681/files#r986001975.

import time
import socket
import re
import select
import errno
import os
import sys
import platform

import subprocess as ssubprocess
import sshuttle.helpers as helpers
from sshuttle.helpers import log, debug1, debug2, debug3, get_env

POLL_TIME = 60 * 15
NETSTAT_POLL_TIME = 30
CACHEFILE = os.path.expanduser('~/.sshuttle.hosts')

# Have we already failed to write CACHEFILE?
CACHE_WRITE_FAILED = False

SHOULD_WRITE_CACHE = False

hostnames = {}
queue = {}
try:
    null = open(os.devnull, 'wb')
except IOError:
    _, e = sys.exc_info()[:2]
    log('warning: %s' % e)
    null = os.popen("sh -c 'while read x; do :; done'", 'wb', 4096)


def _is_ip(s):
    return re.match(r'\d+\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+$', s)


def write_host_cache():
    """If possible, write our hosts file to disk so future connections
       can reuse the hosts that we already found."""
    tmpname = '%s.%d.tmp' % (CACHEFILE, os.getpid())
    global CACHE_WRITE_FAILED
    try:
        f = open(tmpname, 'wb')
        for name, ip in sorted(hostnames.items()):
            f.write(('%s,%s\n' % (name, ip)).encode("ASCII"))
        f.close()
        os.chmod(tmpname, 384)  # 600 in octal, 'rw-------'
        os.rename(tmpname, CACHEFILE)
        CACHE_WRITE_FAILED = False
    except (OSError, IOError):
        # Write message if we haven't yet or if we get a failure after
        # a previous success.
        if not CACHE_WRITE_FAILED:
            log("Failed to write host cache to temporary file "
                "%s and rename it to %s" % (tmpname, CACHEFILE))
            CACHE_WRITE_FAILED = True

        try:
            os.unlink(tmpname)
        except Exception:
            pass


def read_host_cache():
    """If possible, read the cache file from disk to populate hosts that
       were found in a previous sshuttle run."""
    try:
        f = open(CACHEFILE)
    except (OSError, IOError):
        _, e = sys.exc_info()[:2]
        if e.errno == errno.ENOENT:
            return
        else:
            log("Failed to read existing host cache file %s on remote host"
                % CACHEFILE)
            return
    for line in f:
        words = line.strip().split(',')
        if len(words) == 2:
            (name, ip) = words
            name = re.sub(r'[^-\w\.]', '-', name).strip()
            # Remove characters that shouldn't be in IP
            ip = re.sub(r'[^0-9.]', '', ip).strip()
            if name and ip:
                found_host(name, ip)
    f.close()
    global SHOULD_WRITE_CACHE
    if SHOULD_WRITE_CACHE:
        write_host_cache()
        SHOULD_WRITE_CACHE = False


def found_host(name, ip):
    """The provided name maps to the given IP. Add the host to the
       hostnames list, send the host to the sshuttle client via
       stdout, and write the host to the cache file.
    """
    hostname = re.sub(r'\..*', '', name)
    hostname = re.sub(r'[^-\w\.]', '_', hostname)
    if (ip.startswith('127.') or ip.startswith('255.') or
            hostname == 'localhost'):
        return

    if hostname != name:
        found_host(hostname, ip)

    global SHOULD_WRITE_CACHE
    oldip = hostnames.get(name)
    if oldip != ip:
        hostnames[name] = ip
        debug1('Found: %s: %s' % (name, ip))
        sys.stdout.write('%s,%s\n' % (name, ip))
        SHOULD_WRITE_CACHE = True


def _check_etc_hosts():
    """If possible, read /etc/hosts to find hosts."""
    filename = '/etc/hosts'
    debug2(' > Reading %s on remote host' % filename)
    try:
        for line in open(filename):
            line = re.sub(r'#.*', '', line)  # remove comments
            words = line.strip().split()
            if not words:
                continue
            ip = words[0]
            if _is_ip(ip):
                names = words[1:]
                debug3('<    %s %r' % (ip, names))
                for n in names:
                    check_host(n)
                    found_host(n, ip)
    except (OSError, IOError):
        debug1("Failed to read %s on remote host" % filename)


def _check_revdns(ip):
    """Use reverse DNS to try to get hostnames from an IP addresses."""
    debug2(' > rev: %s' % ip)
    try:
        r = socket.gethostbyaddr(ip)
        debug3('<    %s' % r[0])
        check_host(r[0])
        found_host(r[0], ip)
    except (OSError, socket.error, UnicodeError):
        # This case is expected to occur regularly.
        # debug3('<    %s gethostbyaddr failed on remote host' % ip)
        pass


def _check_dns(hostname):
    debug2(' > dns: %s' % hostname)
    try:
        ip = socket.gethostbyname(hostname)
        debug3('<    %s' % ip)
        check_host(ip)
        found_host(hostname, ip)
    except (socket.gaierror, UnicodeError):
        pass


def _check_netstat():
    debug2(' > netstat')
    argv = ['netstat', '-n']
    try:
        p = ssubprocess.Popen(argv, stdout=ssubprocess.PIPE, stderr=null,
                              env=get_env())
        content = p.stdout.read().decode("ASCII")
        p.wait()
    except OSError:
        _, e = sys.exc_info()[:2]
        log('%r failed: %r' % (argv, e))
        return

    # The same IPs may appear multiple times. Consolidate them so the
    # debug message doesn't print the same IP repeatedly.
    ip_list = []
    for ip in re.findall(r'\d+\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+', content):
        if ip not in ip_list:
            ip_list.append(ip)

    for ip in sorted(ip_list):
        debug3('<    %s' % ip)
        check_host(ip)


def check_host(hostname):
    if _is_ip(hostname):
        _enqueue(_check_revdns, hostname)
    else:
        _enqueue(_check_dns, hostname)


def _enqueue(op, *args):
    t = (op, args)
    if queue.get(t) is None:
        queue[t] = 0


def _stdin_still_ok(timeout):
    r, _, _ = select.select([sys.stdin.fileno()], [], [], timeout)
    if r:
        b = os.read(sys.stdin.fileno(), 4096)
        if not b:
            return False
    return True


def hw_main(seed_hosts, auto_hosts):
    helpers.logprefix = 'HH: '

    debug1('Starting hostwatch with Python version %s'
           % platform.python_version())

    for h in seed_hosts:
        check_host(h)

    if auto_hosts:
        read_host_cache()
        _enqueue(_check_etc_hosts)
        _enqueue(_check_netstat)
        check_host('localhost')
        check_host(socket.gethostname())

    while 1:
        now = time.time()
        # For each item in the queue
        for t, last_polled in list(queue.items()):
            (op, args) = t
            if not _stdin_still_ok(0):
                break

            # Determine if we need to run.
            maxtime = POLL_TIME
            # netstat runs more often than other jobs
            if op == _check_netstat:
                maxtime = NETSTAT_POLL_TIME

            # Check if this jobs needs to run.
            if now - last_polled > maxtime:
                queue[t] = time.time()
                op(*args)
            try:
                sys.stdout.flush()
            except IOError:
                break

        # FIXME: use a smarter timeout based on oldest last_polled
        if not _stdin_still_ok(1):  # sleeps for up to 1 second
            break

```
_filter =  models.Q(includes=self) # start with filter clause that always applies
_exclude_filter = models.Q(excludes=self) # also build and exclude
 if self.classes.exists():
    _filter |= Q(product_class__classes=self)
if Product.objects.filter(parent__includes=self).exists():
    _filter |= Q(parent__includes=self)
    _exclude_filter != Q(parent__excludes=self)

# etc ...

return Product.objects.filter(_filter).exclude(_exclude_filter)
```

Everything looks great, but we have one final fix to apply, to the sphinx extension.

See the following screenshot of docs for AuthScopes:
[doc build, scopes page](https://globus-sdk-python--641.org.readthedocs.build/en/641/scopes.html)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1300022/200389564-3927f068-44f4-43a3-a7e8-09eddfd52107.png)

Where did the duplicates come from? (I knew they were there, which is why I went to the doc build. 😉 )

A failing of the `ScopeBuilder` has been fixed. `ScopeBuilder.scope_names` fetches the classattr scopes in addition to the instance-assigned scopes. This was missing in the past mechanism.
The scopes were being explicitly listed with `add_scopes=...`. (I think the classattr list was added after the extension was written.)

The fast fix is to just do this:
```diff
 .. listknownscopes:: globus_sdk.scopes.AuthScopes
-    add_scopes=openid,email,profile
     example_scope=view_identity_set
```

The _optional_ thing to add on here is to remove the `add_scopes=...` feature from the extension, but that can be done in a follow-up. I leave it to your discretion as the PR author whether you'd like us to do that in a separate PR or if you want to include it here.

I think that `makeValidIdentifier` is (and should remain) special purposed to take an identifier and deterministically make it valid. It should not be used for correcting pulumi names. 

Pulumi does not reserve the `pulumi_` namespace, so this is not a reliable way to determine if a package is Pulumi owned. The schema allows you to override package import names, so the only valid way to get an import name is to look at the schema:
https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/1cc5bdc91a2baef50160ac440373354e3041d070/pkg/codegen/nodejs/importer.go#L29-L32

The only way to correctly determine if a package is Pulumi owned is to check the `Package.Publisher == "Pulumi"` field. I'm scared of code that relies on that for correctness though.

Thanks for the PR.

Streamlink already implements the livestream.com plugin (which even got rewritten recently), so the current plugin implementation is pretty much unnecessary and also wrong/incomplete, so I'm not going to comment on the code after the first HTTP request.

Due to the embedding of 3rd party streaming sites on atptour.com, the plugin needs to be turned into a wrapper plugin which just calls `return self.session.streams(url)`.

Adding new plugins which are only wrappers however is always questionable, and it depends on whether the user can get the actual streaming URL from their web browser which could then alternatively be used as Streamlink's input URL. Adding wrapper plugins for external sites overloads Streamlink with useless plugins that (currently) slow down the session initialization, so we want to avoid this. It's also problematic from the perspective of plugin maintenance.

Since there doesn't seem to be a way though to get the actual stream URL from livestream.com via the ATPtour website, I guess adding a wrapper plugin in this case is okay.

----

The following diff should be enough for a wrapper plugin and it fixes some other issues:

```diff

+$description Tennis tournaments organized by the Association of Tennis Professionals.
+$url atptour.com/en/atp-challenger-tour/challenger-tv
 $type live, vod
 """
 
@@ -9,55 +9,22 @@ import re
 
 from streamlink.plugin import Plugin, pluginmatcher
 from streamlink.plugin.api import validate
-from streamlink.stream.hls import HLSStream
 
 log = logging.getLogger(__name__)
 
 
 @pluginmatcher(re.compile(
-    r"https?://(?:www\.)?atptour.com/en/atp-challenger-tour/challenger-tv"
+    r"https?://(?:www\.)?atptour\.com/(?:en|es)/atp-challenger-tour/challenger-tv"
 ))
 class AtpChallengerTour(Plugin):
-    _re_window_config = re.compile(r""".*window.config\s*=\s*(?P<json>{.*?});""", re.DOTALL)
-
     def _get_streams(self):
         iframe_url = self.session.http.get(self.url, schema=validate.Schema(
             validate.parse_html(),

-        ))
-        if not iframe_url:

-                    }

-            ))
+            validate.xml_xpath_string(".//iframe[starts-with(@id,'vimeoPlayer_')][@src][1]/@src"),

         ))

-        stream_url = stream_data['feed']['data'][0]['data']['secure_m3u8_url']
-
-        yield from HLSStream.parse_variant_playlist(self.session, stream_url).items()
+        if iframe_url:
+            return self.session.streams(iframe_url)
 
 
 __plugin__ = AtpChallengerTour

I think it would be interesting to explore how GitPython reacts if the working tree dir is `None` even though the repo isn't bare. Because that seems like it's a bug as the working tree dir should be set even if it's empty.

In other words, non-bare repositories that are empty, i.e. a `.git/` directory in an otherwise empty directory, should not lead to surprises except for being empty.

Note that the main branch might not be born yet on empty repositories, which is something the caller always has to be aware of independently.

Independently of all that, it would definitely help this PR along if there would be more substance to the encountered issues in the form of 'expected - actual' comparisons.

With the addition of a scenario in #4047, I was able to get the following results for this PR (commit 26affbeb50c40454e52b4d88465e670a77c24d08). We see a pretty consistent a worst case increase of 7% (2-3 us) when one of the user agent variants is present in the request headers. This a best case increase of 2-3% when there is no match is important. I would conclude that this PR does not introduce a performance regression. 

| Benchmark                                 | 1.3.2 | 1.4.0rc2.dev81+g26affbeb |
|-------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|-----------------------|
| sethttpmeta-no-useragentvariant           | 33.7 us                              | 34.6 us: 1.03x slower |
| sethttpmeta-all-disabled                  | 13.6 us                              | 14.2 us: 1.04x slower |
| sethttpmeta-useragentvariant_exists_1     | 34.4 us                              | 36.7 us: 1.07x slower |
| sethttpmeta-useragentvariant_exists_2     | 37.4 us                              | 40.2 us: 1.07x slower |
| sethttpmeta-useragentvariant_exists_3     | 34.3 us                              | 36.8 us: 1.07x slower |
| sethttpmeta-useragentvariant_not_exists_1 | 34.3 us                              | 35.2 us: 1.03x slower |
| sethttpmeta-useragentvariant_not_exists_2 | 34.5 us                              | 35.3 us: 1.02x slower                       |
